key_insight: Plastic deformation can be incorporated into contact mechanics by replacing the original surface roughness with an effective power spectrum for plastically modified surfaces.
engineering_relevance: This matters for predicting contact stiffness, electric and thermal contact resistance, and interfacial fluid flow in metal contacts where asperities yield plastically.
method: Derive an effective power spectrum for plastically deformed rough surfaces.
Use the modified spectrum within Persson contact mechanics theory to compute surface height topography.
Compare predicted topography against experiments on steel-on-steel squeezing.
evidence_signal: The paper evaluates the approach by comparing the predicted plastically modified roughness topography with experimental results from Yusof and Ripin.
watch_item: Plastic smoothing may not preserve Gaussian roughness: some surfaces retain a Gaussian-like form with reduced amplitude, while others become strongly skewed after deformation.
